I Now Pronounce You Chuck & Larry
Ice Age: Dawn of the Dinosaurs
How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days
The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo
To All the Boys I've Loved Before
The Worst Person in the World
The Perks of Being a Wallflower
films.review: “(500) Days of Summer,” “Eternal Sunshine,” and “The Perks of Being a Wallflower” due to some intangible similarities, form a personal trilogy for me. They aren’t explicitly connected. My nonchalance towards all of them now, though, feels like a measure of how much I’ve grown from my teenage self.

films.name: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
American Pie Presents: The Naked Mile
American Pie Presents: The Book of Love
Bridget Jones: The Edge of Reason
The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift
G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra
Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows
All Quiet on the Western Front
Baraka
films.review: Roger Ebert exclaimed, "If man sends another Voyager to the distant stars and it can carry only one film on board, that film might be Baraka." The remark astutely represents the film.

films.name: Columbus
films.review: A film that starts slow & unprospectful completely surprises midway and makes you concede that you're in the presence of a rare filmic phenomenon.

films.name: Before Midnight
films.review:
The slowest and dullest of the trilogy, which inadvertently breaks a few of the unstated rules of the narrative structure established in the first two films still is the most enduring and memorable of the three. It goes maybe to the fierce, unbridled honesty of the film, whereas the other two could be chalked up to heartwarming fiction. 
Linklater ends the film on the most beautiful of conundrums, giving solace to optimists while making sure that realists understand that nothing is far from being alright.

films.name: Joram
films.review:
A filmmaker who went bankrupt making films like this clearly has a lot of grit and conviction in what he wants to say. It's one of the very few contemporary films made in complete disregard of the commercial machinery looming over cinema, and the novelty of its approach is proof of that. I mean, who else would make a film that tackles tribal Naxalism without reducing it to easy binaries?
However hard-hitting Joram was, it also felt somewhat forgettable, and unlike Kashyap, Makhija still seems to be struggling to carve out a niche audience of his own.

films.name: The Strange Thing About the Johnsons
Anatomy of a Fall
films.review: The film makes you aware of the ability of the filmmaker to manipulate the audience. Audience's opinion of the fall would shift by the mere tweaking of the tools that makeup what cinema is. Instead of revealing the mystery, the director makes you aware of the fallacy that story-telling is. And that you can never escape the fact that no matter what you do, you can never really know the truth. No matter how hard you study it's Anatomy you'll never unravel the truth of the Fall.

films.name: Blue Valentine
films.review: blue valentine, marriage story & before midnight - could be sorted into the same club for being truthfully honest about what a relationship is; there's some unexpected comfort in thinking about them in retrospect that somehow bypasses all the pain within them.

films.name: Cigarettes & Coffee
films.review:
Three narratives connected with the thread of a 20 dollar bill & surrounded by the tradition of cigarettes & coffee. The film outdoes itself in terms of the cinematography, editing & storyline each aiding in the interleaving of the plots in the minimal scope of a diner on a freeway. PTA leaves us with opaque character motivations resulting the viewer to make sense of the hidden innuendos throughout the film. 
Dialogues seems to come out of a template American screenplay in which gravitas is emphasised more than the substance. The dramatization & structurization makes for an interesting short however it's intentions and conclusions are quite elusive.

films.name: Kartaa
films.review:
The themes of cynicism, dysphoria and disparity in Randeep Jha's latest works like Kohrra and Trail By Fire can be traced down even to his directorial debut Kartaa. 
The film centers around a swindling proletarian property dealer as he struggles with the challenges of his vocation and relations. The short in a pigeon-hole perspective is a plethora of universal relatable problems and is a dystopian take on it. The influence of his predecessor Anurag Kashyap is clearly visible in his vision and portrayal of despair in the society.

films.name: Ajeeb Daastaans
films.review:
The advent of anthology in the Bollywood cine-scope started from the famous 2013's Bombay Talkies with the collaboration of four up-and-coming directors. They later reunited again for Lust Stories & Ghost Stories. Since then more than a few anthologies has been released featuring potential upcoming and sometimes seasoned directors. It's a perfect medium to encourage new talent by accommodating creative freedom and a support system, something which is quite elusive in the mainstream profit-oriented cinema. 
Ajeeb Daastaans is a four film anthology  based on the theme of relationships that hinge on shaky grounds and their inevitable collapse.

Majnu by Shashank Khaitan is definitely the odd film in this anthology, relying more on cast's star power and the grandeur set design. The premise of the film is juvenile and dramatized to a place that it looses it’s significance and becomes just a kitschy portrayal relying on sensuality and bold unintelligible dialogues, even the storyline was too crammed leading to a cheap climax. The film refreshes the memory of Karan Johar’s Lust Stories anthology in terms of it being a kitschy odd fish.

Khilauna by Raj Mehta deals with the tussle between the rich class and their domestic workers. Although the elements of the subject matter are not fleshed the concept of both classes exploiting each other with whatever power at their behest, resorting even to flirtation is fascinating. Deceit and sensuality remain the central elements of the film questioning the position of the characters in the society and the society itself. The conclusion of the film is thrilling but half-cooked in terms of the narrative.

Geeli Pucchi by Neeraj Ghaywan deals with the discrimination based on casteism, treatment of women in conservative Indian households and homosexuality outside the bubble of cinematic dramatization created by the mainstream movies. The love affair between a married brahmin woman with a low-caste factory worker ensues and progresses as they struggle to come to terms with their realities. The film wins your heart with it’s sensitivity and breaks it with the pessimistic conclusion. The film reminds me of the recently released anthology short directed by Konkona Sen herself, The Mirror.

Ankahi by Kayose Irani is a film about the mother of a deaf going child and the turmoil she’s facing as the father can’t come to terms with the reality. She finds solace in another deaf artist and maintains a relationship skirting on the lines of adultery. Most of the conversation in the film is in sign language. The silence all through the film puts an added expectation on the actors to hold the scene which Shefali Shah and Manav Kaul excelled in.

films.name: Kho Gaye Hum Kahan
films.review:
A film that is corresponded with the likes of Dil Chahta Hai & Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara sure does fall a little short of those classics but is fresh and one of the most relevant subjects of the decade. All in all the film is a mixed bag with it nailing the aesthetics and world building revolving around the contemporary present day influencer culture.  The credit for the impressive performances goes to the actors as well as the commendable casting choice of the lead characters.

The film does surely lack the longevity of the predecessor classics and fails in imbuing the same hopeful and inspirational message that makes them a go-to feel good staple. But that does speaks also to the present attention span lacking and content consuming era we are in.

The conflicts of the film feel half-baked and could’ve been a little more immaculate. The conclusion of the second half was also somewhat hasty resulting in the movie coming off a little preachy. 

The vibes surely live up to the characteristic Zoya Akhtar benchmark and is a great feel-good piece of contemporary film.

films.name: Dunki
films.review:
Fact that Indian cinema audience is at the height of stratification is apparent if you observe the top earning cinema hall movies. With a mature audience been exposed to finer ideas & world cinema finding their niche on streaming platforms the percent of underexposed audience surely has increased in cinema hall, that is quite apparent in the patterns observed in the top grossers in the Post-COVID era. With simplistic thrill-packed or star-powered formula films topping the charts ubiquitously. The treatment of various issues in these films have been superficial and oversimplified. On one hand the Indian audience is becoming more concious and demanding due to the accessible content consumption, on the other a huge section is completely oblivious to the changing era and enjoys the same if not even more rudimentary concepts and ideas. That creates a dichotomy for cinema producers as to, how and what films to create?
This confusion and struggle is evident in Dunki too. The subject of the film was potent but the treatment did not live upto Hirani's standards. Instead of the characters serving the film it felt that the movie was serving the actors, especially SRK. Which was unlike every Hirani movie. That in a line is everything that was lacking in Hirani's latest production.

films.name: Fursat
films.review:
One of the most experimental films produced in mainstream India, it feels like an art museum production, pushing the boundaries of cinema and storytelling. It amalgamates dance, musicals, science fiction, and Indian heritage through a crisscrossing narrative and opera-like performances.
It's a classic representation of Vishal Bhardwaj's artistic sensibilities — a liberated contemporary approach where science fiction is immersed in Indian culture, be it literature, folklore, architecture, and, most importantly, his music. The use of body performance as a storytelling device felt completely novel within the mainstream Indian landscape.
Wamiqa Gabbi truly nails the role becoming a staple Vishal Bhardwaj choice in his later productions. That said, the film does become a little incoherent at times and somewhat loses its resolution amidst all the innovation.

films.name: Ramprasad Ki Tehrvi
films.review:
Exploration of realism is scarce in Indian Cinema for it's considered mundane and uneventful. Ramprasad ki Tehrvi is a great rendition of the parallel alt cinema, centred around the 13 day event after one's death. It astutely points out the inter-relational dynamics along with the politics, back-bitching and hypocrisy of it all veiled under the constructs of courtesy and civility. All centred around the death of their father pointing out the irony and futility of life & relations.

It's Seema Pahwa's directorial debut based on the adaptation of her own play Pind Daan. The film did a great job reflecting the conventional, stereotypical joint familial associations.

films.name: Dhak Dhak
films.review:
Four women from different societal sects come together for a trip to Khardung La. This film is one of the best portrayal of feministic liberation seen in Bollywood in a long time. It candidly points and strives against an umbrella of communal facets hindering women without being propogandic. It's an entertaining wholesome movie, being inspiring and self-reflective depending on where you stand. 
The technique and narrative fumbles at a few places especially during the second half but the film is commendable considering it's the director Tarun Dudeja's feature debut.

films.name: Yuva
films.review:
This was my first exposure to Mani Ratnam. I was always aware of his reputation, but never quite understood it until now. Watching this felt like experiencing a more dynamic Kiarostami or Farhadi film — incredibly precise in technique, yet deeply rooted in a beautiful, unadorned Indian aesthetic with a quiet sense of poetry and romanticism.
The way the three narratives criss-cross and gradually come together is immaculate. Based on this one film alone, I find his sensibility and visual taste more compelling than Bhansali's, and even Bhardwaj's to an extent. The authenticity of his world-building rivals some of the best work from Bhardwaj and Kashyap.
Beyond the performances and chemistry, the dialogues are among the best I've seen. It made perfect sense when I later discovered they were written by Kashyap — he's truly exceptional at it. Every lead performance is fantastic, with the only weak link being Esha Deol, whose chemistry with Ajay Devgn doesn't quite land.

films.name: WHAT DID JACK DO?
films.review:
An inspector interrogates an orangutan caught fleeing a railway station under suspicion of murdering his lover's companion, a hen. The ludicrous premise is both unsettling and comical, functioning almost as a satire of human relationships and the absurdity of our social constructs.
The exchanges between the inspector and the orangutan go in circles, filled with witty yet mildly counterproductive banter, often responding to serious inquiries with idioms and evasions. As the film unfolds, you're constantly torn between treating it as a comedic spectacle or digging deeper for meaning.
One line exchange that stayed with me, and perhaps encapsulates the film's twisted charm, is:
Detective: "Who's gonna believe an orangutan?"
Orangutan: "Maybe a jury, big boy."

films.name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
